zones.name: Analysis Engine
zones.description: The core static-analysis pipeline: file inventory, import graph traversal, zone enrichment, LLM-assisted classification, output generation, and MCP server entry.
insights: 19 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
High cohesion (0.62) with 19 distinct entry points confirms this is the gravitational center of the sourcevision package — most other zones import from here.

The 7 reverse imports (analyzers → cli, analyzers → unit) are a mild inversion concern: a core engine ideally has no upward dependencies into CLI or schema layers; isolating those imports would strengthen the layering.
The zone size (40 files) and entry-point count are large; consider whether sub-zones like 'enrich-pipeline' or 'output-generation' could be split off as the codebase grows.

Reverse imports from analyzers into the cli and unit zones (7 + 5 edges) suggest mild layering inversions; auditing these imports and pushing shared types down to a foundation module would improve the dependency graph.
High cohesion (0.62) and low coupling (0.38) reflect a well-bounded core engine — the majority of cross-zone traffic flows in the correct direction (inward toward this zone).
Zone "analysis-engine" has files across 9 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Receives 77 total inbound import edges across all dependent zones (29 unit + 20 cli + 15 analyzers-2 + 13 analyzers-3) — this extreme fan-in makes any signature change in this zone propagate to all four dependents simultaneously, constituting the highest change-risk surface in the codebase.

Participates in two separate bidirectional cycles (with cli and with unit) that together form a triangular mutual dependency spanning all three primary zones — the pairwise bidirectional observations understate the severity because the triangle means every zone is reachable from every other zone through import chains.

analysis-engine, analyze-pipeline-cli, and branch-workspace-reporting form a triangular circular dependency: analyzers→cli (7), cli→unit (13), unit→analyzers (29), analyzers→unit (5), unit→cli (3), cli→analyzers (20). All three zones are mutually reachable — this is a system-level cycle, not just three isolated pairwise inversions, and cannot be resolved by refactoring any single zone alone.

No explicit facade or public-interface module exists within this zone — all 19 entry points are concrete implementation files. Consumer zones couple directly to implementation details (e.g. enrich-batch.ts, zones.ts) rather than to a stable interface, meaning any internal rename or signature change in this zone has an unconstrained blast radius across all four dependent zones simultaneously.

completion-reader.ts contains a self-documented local copy of parsing logic from branch-work-collector.ts (in the branch-workspace-reporting zone) — this is a cross-zone logic duplication that creates silent divergence risk: if the original parsing logic changes, the copy will not be updated.

completion-reader.ts explicitly documents itself as a local copy of logic from branch-work-collector.ts — duplicated cross-zone logic with no enforcement mechanism to keep the two in sync; any fix to the original will silently miss the copy.

Zone exposes 19 concrete implementation files as its API surface with no facade or interface module — consumer zones are forced to import internal files directly, creating maximum blast radius for any internal restructuring.

Zone name 'analysis-engine' is misaligned with its source directory 'src/analyzers/' — developers must mentally remap the architectural name to the filesystem path every time they navigate, and the Louvain algorithm uses the directory name as a clustering signal, making the mismatch a persistent source of zone boundary confusion.

claude-client.ts (an LLM HTTP client) lives inside the core computation zone with no abstraction boundary — the analysis engine carries a hidden external network dependency that cannot be mocked at the zone boundary, making all unit tests that import from this zone implicitly network-aware unless they mock at the module level.

enrich-config.ts carries a [config] archetype inside the core engine zone — configuration data structures are co-located with computation logic, preventing configuration from being independently loaded or validated without importing the full engine zone.

Zone name 'analysis-engine' does not match its source directory 'src/analyzers/' — align them by either renaming the zone to 'analyzers-core' or restructuring source files under 'src/analysis-engine/' so IDE navigation, zone references in documentation, and Louvain clustering inputs are all consistent.

claude-client.ts provides direct LLM API access from within the core computation zone with no interface abstraction — extract an ILLMClient interface and inject it as a dependency so unit tests can substitute a stub and the analysis engine zone no longer carries an implicit external network dependency.
[call graph] 969 internal calls, 65 outgoing, 246 incoming (cohesion: 0.94, coupling: 0.06)

zones.name: Analyze Pipeline CLI
zones.description: Orchestrates the end-to-end analyze command: phase sequencing, manifest management, convergence detection, git credential handling, PDF export, and all CLI output/error formatting.
insights: Good cohesion (0.65) with low coupling (0.35) indicates a well-bounded CLI orchestration layer that correctly concentrates command logic without leaking into the core engine.

The 13 imports from cli → unit and 20 from cli → analyzers show appropriate directional flow: the CLI layer depends on both the core engine and supporting utilities without creating cycles (except for the 3 unit → cli reverse edges worth investigating).

Including branch-work-collector and convergence detection in this zone alongside pure CLI commands is reasonable — these are pipeline-phase coordinators that belong close to the analyze command entry point.
Zone "analyze-pipeline-cli" has files across 7 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Three reverse imports (unit → cli) create a minor cycle between the CLI and branch-workspace-reporting zones; identifying which unit-zone files import from CLI and moving any shared symbols to a lower layer would eliminate the cycle.
Cohesion of 0.65 is the strongest among the non-trivial zones, confirming that the CLI orchestration layer is well-organized around the analyze pipeline lifecycle.
git-credential-helper.ts and export-pdf.ts are ancillary features within the CLI zone; if they grow in complexity they are good candidates for extraction into dedicated feature zones.

Despite having the highest cohesion score (0.65), this zone is still a full participant in both a pairwise cycle with branch-workspace-reporting and the triangular system-level cycle — cohesion measures internal structure only; a high cohesion score provides no guarantee of clean external boundaries.

High internal cohesion (0.65) coexists with active participation in circular dependencies — cohesion and external coupling are orthogonal properties; the CLI zone's boundary health requires coupling analysis, not cohesion metrics, to diagnose.

branch-work-collector.ts and convergence.ts are domain-layer services (data collection, delta computation) that live inside the CLI zone purely because they are called by CLI commands. Their presence means any non-CLI consumer (e.g. a future API or agent) must import through the CLI zone to reach these services, inverting the intended dependency direction.

pr-markdown.ts imports branch-work-collector.ts directly from the analyzers path — this is a CLI command file reaching across into a domain service that arguably belongs to the branch-workspace-reporting zone, creating an undeclared cross-zone dependency that bypasses zone boundaries.

branch-work-collector.ts and convergence.ts are domain services (not CLI commands) placed in the CLI zone — non-CLI consumers cannot reach these services without taking a transitive dependency on the CLI orchestration layer.

pr-markdown.ts (CLI command) directly imports branch-work-collector.ts from the analyzers path while branch-work-collector lives in the analyze-pipeline-cli zone — the import path and the zone boundary are misaligned, hiding that this is a CLI-to-CLI intra-zone call rather than a CLI-to-domain cross-zone call.

export-pdf.ts and git-credential-helper.ts are ancillary utilities with no semantic connection to the analyze pipeline lifecycle — their presence is a zone naming violation: the zone promises 'analyze-pipeline' semantics but hosts unrelated CLI commands, inflating file count and weakening the zone's semantic coherence signal.

convergence.ts computes stateless algorithmic deltas (pure function, no side effects) — pure computation functions should live at the lowest possible architectural tier rather than inside the CLI orchestration zone where they are only reachable by consuming the entire CLI layer.

convergence.ts is a stateless pure-function algorithm placed inside the CLI orchestration zone — move it to the analysis-engine zone or a shared foundation module so its logic is reachable without a CLI layer dependency, and the CLI zone's semantic boundary remains limited to command orchestration.

export-pdf.ts and git-credential-helper.ts have no relationship to the analyze pipeline lifecycle — their presence violates the zone's stated semantic scope; move them to an 'ancillary-cli-commands' zone or equivalent to preserve the zone's architectural focus and prevent the 'analyze-pipeline' name from becoming misleading.
[call graph] 502 internal calls, 45 outgoing, 36 incoming (cohesion: 0.92, coupling: 0.08)

zones.name: Branch Workspace Reporting
zones.description: Groups branch-work tracking utilities, risk scoring, workspace aggregation, PR report generation, schema definitions, and the package public API alongside co-located unit tests.
insights: High coupling (0.6) — 29 imports target "analysis-engine"

Coupling at exactly 0.6 (the warning threshold) and borderline cohesion (0.4) indicate this zone is a catch-all for files that didn't cluster tightly elsewhere — schema types, generators, CLI commands, and tests all cohabit here.

The presence of src/public.ts (the package entrypoint) in this zone alongside tests/unit/ helpers suggests the community-detection algorithm grouped files by co-import density rather than by semantic role; a deliberate split of schema, generators, and tests into separate zones would improve clarity.
The '-tests' suffix must not be applied here because production files (public.ts, v1.ts, branch-work-*.ts, risk-scoring.ts) significantly outnumber the test helpers.

Coupling score of 0.6 meets the warning threshold; the zone imports from analyzers (29), cli (3), analyzers-2 (2), analyzers-3 (3), and e2e (2), indicating it acts as a cross-cutting hub rather than a focused module.

Mixing src/public.ts (the package's public API entrypoint), schema type files, and unit test helpers in one community suggests the Louvain graph does not yet have enough edge weight to separate these concerns; manually anchoring schema and public API into a dedicated zone would help.

Branch-work utilities (classifier, filter, store) and risk-scoring form a coherent sub-cluster within this zone; they could be extracted into a dedicated 'branch-risk-analysis' zone if the codebase grows.
Zone "branch-workspace-reporting" has files across 10 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

The 13 CLI imports targeting this zone (cli→unit) reach production branch-work utilities that share a zone with test helpers — the CLI layer therefore has a transitive compile-time dependency on test scaffolding, making it impossible to tree-shake test utilities from production builds without first splitting the zone.

Production utilities (branch-work-*.ts, risk-scoring.ts) cohabiting with test helpers in the same zone causes the analyze-pipeline-cli zone to transitively depend on test infrastructure through its 13 inbound imports — separating production utilities from test helpers would break this unwanted coupling.

The zone hosts branch-work-collector.ts (imported by CLI pr-markdown.ts) and a local-copy duplicate exists in completion-reader.ts (analysis-engine zone) — the zone owns the canonical version of parsing logic that has already been forked once, indicating it will be forked again as consumers avoid taking the full zone dependency.

branch-work-collector.ts is the canonical owner of parsing logic that has already been duplicated into analysis-engine/completion-reader.ts — hosting authoritative domain logic in a high-coupling catch-all zone creates pressure to fork rather than import, accelerating divergence.

This zone scores BOTH high coupling (0.6) AND low cohesion (0.4) simultaneously — the combination is the canonical fragile-zone pattern: high coupling means changes in neighbor zones propagate inward freely, and low cohesion means the zone has no internal structure to absorb those changes safely. No other zone in this batch exhibits both conditions at once, making this zone the highest structural fragility risk in the codebase.

src/public.ts (the package public API entrypoint) living in this high-coupling zone means any downstream consumer who imports the package's public API silently acquires a transitive coupling to branch-workspace internals — there is no way to import the public API without depending on this zone's full transitive closure.

This zone has BOTH high coupling (0.6) AND low cohesion (0.4) — the only zone in this batch to exhibit both simultaneously. Prioritize splitting: move src/public.ts and schema types to a dedicated zone, and move branch-work utilities to a 'branch-risk-analysis' zone, to break the fragile catch-all structure before further features are added.

src/public.ts co-located with branch-workspace internals forces every package consumer to transitively couple to this high-coupling zone — extract public.ts to its own dedicated zone (e.g. 'package-public-api') so the public API surface is independent of the branch reporting internals and can be changed without affecting branch-workspace coupling metrics.
[call graph] 1361 internal calls, 277 outgoing, 614 incoming (cohesion: 0.83, coupling: 0.17)

zones.name: Claude Tool Config
zones.description: Stores Claude Code permission settings and local overrides for the n-dx development environment.
insights: Isolated files — no import edges between 2 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
Settings files are not imported by any production code, making this zone a pure configuration island with no graph edges.
Splitting global and local settings into two files follows good practice for keeping sensitive or machine-specific config out of version control.
No action required — this zone has no architectural role beyond IDE tooling configuration.
Zero cohesion and coupling scores reflect that these JSON files have no import relationships; this is expected and correct for IDE config files.

zones.name: File Analyzer Implementations
zones.description: Houses the concrete analyzer service implementations — callgraph, component, import, inventory, and route-detection — along with their unit tests and the shared path utilities they depend on.
insights: High coupling (0.52) — 15 imports target "analysis-engine"
The 15 imports into the 'Analysis Engine' zone signal that these analyzers share a common foundation; audit whether shared utilities could be consolidated to reduce cross-zone coupling.
route-detection.ts sitting alongside general-purpose analyzers suggests it may belong in its own focused zone once zone boundaries are tightened.
Unit tests are co-located in this zone, which is good for discoverability but inflates the coupling metric — test-only imports should not penalize production cohesion scores.

Zone cohesion (0.48) and coupling (0.52) are moderate; the zone is a natural grouping of concrete analyzer services but would benefit from extracting route-detection into its own zone given its distinct domain.
Heavy inbound imports from cli (5) and e2e (3) zones indicate these analyzers are a key dependency surface — their public APIs should be kept stable and well-documented.
route-detection.ts shares no import edges with the other analyzers in this zone — it is co-located by directory convention only, not by actual coupling, making it a false positive in zone grouping.

The zone exports 15 imports into analysis-engine but receives only 2 back — this is a clean consumer relationship, not a bidirectional entanglement; the high coupling metric is directional and not a circular dependency risk.

route-detection.ts has no import edges to sibling analyzer files in this zone; it is grouped by filesystem proximity only and should be extracted to a dedicated route-analysis zone once zone boundaries are tightened.
The 15 outbound imports to analysis-engine and 2 inbound form a clean one-directional consumer relationship — no circular dependency exists between these two zones.

src/util/paths.ts is classified as a utility and lives in src/util/ but is grouped into this zone purely by import affinity — it is a shared utility, not an analyzer implementation, and its presence inflates the zone's file count while obscuring its cross-cutting role.

The zone contains both production analyzer services and their unit tests but no dedicated test-support helpers — any test setup logic is likely duplicated across the three test files, creating a hidden maintenance surface.

src/util/paths.ts is a utility misclassified into the analyzer-implementations zone by import affinity. As a cross-cutting utility imported by multiple zones, it belongs in a dedicated foundation or utility zone alongside sort.ts and schema/index.ts — the three hub utilities should share an owner zone.

src/analyzers/index.ts is classified as an entrypoint and lives in this zone, but entrypoints normally belong to the zone that owns the public API surface — having the analyzer barrel export inside the implementations zone rather than the analysis-engine zone means the public API has no stable owner separate from the concrete implementations.

The zone name 'file-analyzer-implementations' implies corresponding interfaces or abstractions exist elsewhere, but no interface or abstract-class zone exists in the codebase — the 'implementations' suffix is misleading naming that implies a contract layer that has never been created.

src/analyzers/index.ts (entrypoint archetype) belongs in the analysis-engine zone that owns the public API, not the implementations zone — relocating it would give the public API surface a clear owner and prevent the entrypoint from being refactored as if it were a private implementation detail.

Rename the zone from 'file-analyzer-implementations' to 'file-analyzers' or 'analyzer-services' — the 'implementations' suffix implies a corresponding interface layer that does not exist, creating a misleading architectural signal for anyone onboarding to the codebase.
[call graph] 384 internal calls, 7 outgoing, 34 incoming (cohesion: 0.98, coupling: 0.02)

zones.name: Project Root
zones.description: Houses workspace-level documentation, build configuration, and project identity assets that define the monorepo structure.
insights: Isolated files — no import edges between 9 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)

Documentation files (ARCHITECTURE.md, WORKSPACE_DESIGN.md, README.md) live alongside build tooling (tsconfig, vitest.config, package.json), indicating this zone is a documentation-plus-config layer rather than a code layer.
Having zero imports in/out is correct — root config should never be imported by source modules.
The presence of image assets (SourceVision-F.png, SourceVision.png) in the root zone suggests no dedicated assets directory; consider a docs/ or assets/ folder if the asset count grows.
Zero cohesion and coupling are expected for a root config zone; no structural changes needed.
Image assets mixed with markdown and build config files is a minor organization concern; a dedicated assets/ or docs/ directory would improve navigability.

zones.name: Schema Validation
zones.description: Contains the JSON schema validation module for sourcevision output files, alongside the end-to-end CLI tests and integration pipeline tests that exercise the full analysis workflow.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

src/schema/validate.ts is the only production file here; the community detection algorithm grouped it with e2e/integration tests likely because validate.ts is only exercised in full-pipeline scenarios rather than unit tests — verify it is also called during the core analysis flow.

Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling indicate this group is internally self-consistent but isolated from the broader import graph — the production module may be underutilized if it isn't imported from the main analysis pipeline.
E2e and integration tests are co-located in this zone; consider whether separating them by test type would improve test organization and CI parallelism.

The production file src/schema/validate.ts is co-located with e2e and integration tests, suggesting it is only exercised at the pipeline level — verify it is also called during the core analysis flow to avoid silent validation gaps.
Zero coupling to other zones is architecturally clean for a validation module; this makes schema validation portable and easy to test in isolation.

Zero coupling to the analysis-engine zone means the schema validation step is never called from within the pipeline's own import graph — validation is only exercised at the e2e level, creating a silent gap where partial or malformed outputs can be written without any in-process validation.

The co-location of e2e and integration test files with the sole production file (validate.ts) in one Louvain community confirms that validate.ts is never imported from production pipeline code — this is a structural signal, not a naming coincidence.

validate.ts has zero import edges to or from any production zone; it is only exercised through e2e and integration tests. If the analysis pipeline does not call validate() at the end of each run, malformed output files can reach consumers silently.

The schema-validation zone is correctly isolated from analyzers and cli zones for portability, but the absence of any inbound import from the pipeline entrypoint means validation is opt-in rather than enforced — a single import from the pipeline finalization step would close this gap.

The zone name 'schema-validation' is misleading: 4 of 5 files are test files (e2e and integration), making this a test-grouping artifact rather than a schema domain zone — the zone label should reflect its actual composition.

The co-presence of both e2e tests (cli-analyze, cli-serve) and an integration test (pipeline.test.ts) in one Louvain community suggests these test tiers share a common import target (validate.ts) but have different execution scopes — they should not be treated as the same architectural tier for CI parallelism purposes.

Zone contains 1 production file and 4 test files (80% test) — the zone boundary is a test-grouping artifact, not a domain boundary. The single production file (validate.ts) has no identity as a 'zone' and should be absorbed into a shared schema or foundation zone where it can be imported by the pipeline.

E2e and integration test files are co-located in the same Louvain community because both import validate.ts — CI parallelism and test reporting depend on these tiers being separately categorized. Mixing them in one zone obscures which test tier caught a regression.

A cohesion score of 1.0 on a zone with only one production file is mathematically inevitable regardless of architectural quality — any single production file co-located with tests that import only it will score 1.0. The metric is not meaningful at this zone size and should not be interpreted as an architectural health signal.

The zone name 'schema-validation' describes only the single production file; 80% of the zone's files are tests. Naming a zone after one production file while 4 test files determine its Louvain membership makes the zone label actively misleading in status reports and architectural diagrams.

Cohesion=1.0 for a single-file production zone is a degenerate metric, not an architectural achievement — sourcevision should suppress or annotate cohesion scores for zones with fewer than 3 production files to prevent false confidence in zone health.

Absorb validate.ts into a shared 'schema' or 'foundation' zone alongside schema/index.ts so the production file gains a meaningful owner zone, and move the e2e/integration tests back to their respective test-tier zones — this eliminates the misleading zone name and fixes the mixed-tier test co-location in one step.
[call graph] 87 internal calls, 10 outgoing, 11 incoming (cohesion: 0.9, coupling: 0.1)

zones.name: Test Fixtures
zones.description: Provides static sample projects (a Remix app and a small TypeScript project) used as realistic inputs for end-to-end and integration tests of the analysis pipeline.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling confirm this zone is completely self-contained — test fixtures correctly have no runtime dependencies on production code.

Two fixture types (Remix app for route-detection, small-ts-project for general analysis) cover the two main analysis scenarios; a third fixture with circular imports or a large-scale graph could improve edge-case coverage.
Fixture files should be kept minimal and purpose-built; avoid letting them drift into realistic project copies that become expensive to maintain over time.
All 11 files are test fixtures with no production code — the zone is cleanly isolated and serves as stable input data for the test suite.
The Remix fixture covers route-detection scenarios specifically; ensure it stays synchronized with supported Remix conventions as the route-detection analyzer evolves.
Zone "test-fixtures" has files across 5 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

The two fixture projects serve orthogonal test concerns (Remix route-detection vs generic TS import graph) and have no shared files — they are two independent test datasets grouped together only by filesystem nesting under tests/fixtures/.

No production zone imports from test-fixtures, confirming correct dependency direction; however, if fixture paths are hardcoded in test files rather than resolved via a shared constant, fixture relocation will require multi-file edits.

The two fixture sub-projects (remix-app, small-ts-project) are unrelated datasets with no shared imports; grouping them in one zone is a filesystem artifact. If a third fixture is added, consider a fixtures/index.ts manifest to keep fixture discovery centralized.

Zero inbound edges from any production zone confirms correct isolation; verify that fixture paths are referenced via a shared constant in test helpers rather than duplicated path strings to reduce coupling to the filesystem layout.

The two fixture sub-projects serve orthogonal concerns (Remix route-detection vs generic TS import graph analysis) but share the same zone — if either fixture grows to support new test scenarios, there is no structural mechanism to prevent cross-fixture import leakage.

No shared fixture-loader or path-constants file exists in the test-fixtures zone — fixture paths are likely hardcoded as strings in test files across multiple zones, meaning any fixture directory rename requires multi-file edits with no single point of control.

Fixture paths are consumed as string literals scattered across test files in multiple zones rather than through a centralized fixture registry or path-constants module. A single tests/fixtures/index.ts exporting named fixture paths would make relocation atomic and prevent path drift.

The fixture set covers only two scenarios (Remix route-detection and small-ts generic imports) but the analyzers under test include callgraph, components, zones, and imports analysis — fixture coverage is asymmetric, with route-detection over-represented relative to the codebase's total analyzer surface.

The two fixture sub-projects use different file extensions (.tsx for Remix, .ts for small-ts) but share the same zone; if a new component-analysis fixture is added using .tsx, it will be indistinguishable from the Remix route-detection fixture by extension alone — fixture purpose should be encoded in directory naming convention, not inferred from extension.

Add a fixture-to-analyzer coverage matrix (even as a comment in tests/fixtures/index.ts) mapping each fixture to the analyzers it exercises — currently there is no documented link between fixture files and the analyzer features they cover, making it unclear when adding a new analyzer feature whether a new fixture is needed.

Establish a naming convention that encodes fixture purpose in the directory name (e.g. tests/fixtures/route-detection-remix/, tests/fixtures/import-graph-ts/) rather than relying on implicit knowledge — the current names 'remix-app' and 'small-ts-project' describe technology stack, not the analyzer scenario being exercised.
[call graph] 5 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

zones.name: Zone Detection Engine
zones.description: Implements the Louvain community-detection algorithm, zone hashing for stability, and the zone assembly pipeline that transforms the import graph into named architectural zones.
insights: Low cohesion (0.27) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
High coupling (0.73) — 13 imports target "analysis-engine"
Low cohesion (0.27) signals that louvain.ts, zone-hash.ts, and zones.ts may not be tightly coupled to each other — consider whether zone-hash.ts belongs closer to the output or schema layer.

High coupling (0.73) combined with 13 imports from the main Analysis Engine zone suggests this zone is a dependent extension rather than an independent subsystem; merging with 'Analysis Engine' may improve clarity.
The three zone-detection unit tests provide good coverage of a complex algorithmic area, but test files inflating the coupling metric may obscure the true production dependency structure.

Cohesion of 0.27 is below the 0.4 threshold — the three production files (louvain.ts, zone-hash.ts, zones.ts) may not be tightly enough related to warrant their own zone; merging with the 'Analysis Engine' zone should be evaluated.

Coupling of 0.73 exceeds the 0.6 warning threshold, indicating heavy dependency on other zones; the zone-detection logic is not self-contained and relies significantly on the broader analysis infrastructure.

zone-hash.ts has no documented consumers outside this zone, yet it contributes to the coupling metric — if it is only used internally it may be a private implementation detail incorrectly surfaced as a zone boundary file.

The three unit test files in this zone cover distinct algorithmic concerns (detection, size policy, subdivision) suggesting the production logic has already been implicitly split into three sub-domains that have not yet been reflected in source organization.

Three separate unit test files (zone-detection, zone-size-policy, zone-subdivision) map to distinct sub-domains within two production files — the test surface reveals a hidden split that should be made explicit in source organization before these concerns grow further.

zone-hash.ts has no outbound edges to zones outside zone-detection-engine; if it is only consumed within this zone it is a private module incorrectly treated as a zone boundary and should be marked internal.

zone-hash.ts has no outbound import edges to other zones and no inbound edges from zones outside zone-detection-engine — if confirmed, it is a private implementation detail that should not influence zone boundary calculations or be surfaced as a public API file.

The zone has equal numbers of production files (louvain.ts, zone-hash.ts, zones.ts) and unit test files (zone-detection, zone-size-policy, zone-subdivision) — a 1:1 ratio suggests good test coverage, but the three test files covering distinct algorithmic sub-domains (detection, size policy, subdivision) reveal that zones.ts is doing too much and should be split along those same sub-domain lines.

zones.ts is exercised by three separate unit test files each covering a distinct algorithmic concern (detection, size policy, subdivision) — this indicates zones.ts has at least three separate responsibilities. Splitting it into zones-detect.ts, zones-size-policy.ts, and zones-subdivide.ts would align source organization with the already-expressed test structure and improve zone cohesion from 0.27.

zone-detection-engine is the only zone in the codebase with BOTH low cohesion (0.27) AND high coupling (0.73) simultaneously — this combination defines a fragile zone: its files are loosely related to each other but tightly dependent on external zones, meaning a change to any neighbor can break the zone while the zone itself provides no clear encapsulation benefit.

The 'engine' suffix in the zone name implies autonomy and self-containment, but a coupling score of 0.73 directly contradicts this — a zone with 73% of its import weight crossing its own boundary is a module, not an engine. The name should reflect the zone's actual role as a dependent sub-module of the analysis pipeline.

zone-detection-engine has both low cohesion (0.27) and high coupling (0.73) — this is the highest-fragility zone in the codebase. Any refactor of the analysis-engine zone will cascade here. Prioritize merging louvain.ts and zone-hash.ts into the analysis-engine zone or extracting them into a named sub-package before any broader architectural work begins.

Rename the zone to 'zone-detection' or 'louvain-clustering' to accurately reflect its dependent sub-module role — 'engine' implies autonomy that the coupling metrics explicitly contradict, and the misleading name may cause developers to treat it as a stable boundary when it is not.

The 'analyzers' zone is the correct architectural hub with the highest in-degree; all other zones importing from it follows the intended dependency direction.

Bidirectional import edges between analyzers↔cli (7 edges) and analyzers↔unit (5 edges) indicate that some shared types or utilities currently live in the wrong zone — pushing them down into a shared foundation module would eliminate these inversions.

The three sibling 'analyzers-2' and 'analyzers-3' overflow zones (not in this batch) together with the main analyzers zone suggest the core engine has grown beyond what Louvain can cluster into a single community; a deliberate refactor into named sub-packages (e.g. enrich, output, classify) would produce more meaningful zone boundaries.

Unit test files appearing in the 'branch-workspace-reporting' zone rather than in a dedicated test zone suggests test files are tightly co-imported with production utilities — collocating tests with source is fine, but it makes zone naming and archetype classification harder.
Overall coupling across the five zones is healthy: the primary flow is many-zones → analysis-engine, which is the correct hub-and-spoke pattern for a pipeline architecture.

The three 'analyzers-*' zones (Analysis Engine, File Analyzer Implementations, Zone Detection Engine) form a tightly coupled cluster with 30+ cross-zone imports between them — their algorithmic separation suggests sub-optimal boundaries; a manual zone review should consider consolidating into one or two zones with clearer domain ownership.

The 'Zone Detection Engine' zone (analyzers-3) has both low cohesion and high coupling, making it the highest-priority candidate for architectural attention — either merging into 'Analysis Engine' or refactoring zone-hash.ts into a shared utility.

Bidirectional imports between 'Analysis Engine' and 'Branch Workspace Reporting' (analyzers→unit: 5, unit→analyzers: 29) should be audited to confirm no runtime circular dependencies exist — this pattern is acceptable if the unit→analyzers direction is test-only.

The 'Schema Validation' zone's isolation from the main analyzer cross-zone import cluster is noteworthy — if schema validation is not invoked from within the pipeline itself, output files may be produced without validation, creating a silent reliability gap.

Seven import edges flowing from the core analysis-engine zone upward into cli and unit zones are the most significant architectural risk in this batch; resolving them would enforce a strict layered dependency graph.

The branch-workspace-reporting zone's 0.6 coupling score combined with its role as the public API host makes it the highest-priority zone to refactor — splitting schema types, branch-work utilities, and public.ts into separate zones would reduce coupling and clarify the public API surface.
The analyze-pipeline-cli zone's high cohesion (0.65) and low coupling (0.35) make it the cleanest zone in the batch and a good structural reference for how other zones should be organized.
Zero-edge zones (.claude, root) are correctly isolated from all source code; no action needed for these configuration zones.

Three analyzer zones (Analysis Engine, File Analyzer Implementations, Zone Detection Engine) show 30+ cross-zone imports forming a dense cluster; these zones likely share enough coupling to benefit from a manual merge or boundary review.
Bidirectional cross-zone imports between 'Analysis Engine' and 'Branch Workspace Reporting' (analyzers↔unit) should be audited to confirm they are test-only and not runtime circular dependencies.
The test fixtures zone is cleanly isolated with perfect cohesion, setting a good example for how test support infrastructure should be structured.

Overall the codebase shows clear separation between CLI orchestration, analysis engines, and test infrastructure — the main architectural risk is within the analyzer cluster where zone boundaries do not yet reflect clear domain ownership.

src/analyzers/ contains files from 5 zones (analysis-engine, analyze-pipeline-cli, branch-workspace-reporting, file-analyzer-implementations, zone-detection-engine) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries
File "src/util/paths.ts" has 89% of import neighbors in src/analyzers/ but lives in src/util/ — consider relocating

File "tests/fixtures/small-ts-project/src/models/user.ts" has 100% of import neighbors in tests/fixtures/small-ts-project/src/services/ but lives in tests/fixtures/small-ts-project/src/models/ — consider relocating

The three hub files (zones.ts, schema/index.ts, sort.ts) are each imported by 4 separate zones but have no dedicated foundation zone to host them — their cross-cutting reach means any API change ripples to all four dependents simultaneously; a dedicated 'core' or 'foundation' zone owning these files would reduce implicit coupling and make the dependency surface explicit.

The triangular circular dependency (analysis-engine ↔ analyze-pipeline-cli ↔ branch-workspace-reporting) means the three primary zones are all mutually reachable — this is qualitatively more severe than three independent pairwise inversions because it prevents any safe incremental extraction without first breaking the cycle at a shared boundary.

The e2e zone (cohesion 1.0, correctly targeted at the file-analyzer layer, no imports from core engine) is the cleanest non-config zone in the codebase and can serve as a structural reference for how test zones should isolate their concerns.

The only safe import paths in the current graph are: config zones → nothing (correct), e2e → analyzers-2 (acceptable test→impl direction), and analyzers-2/3 → analysis-engine (correct sibling→core direction) — all other cross-zone edges either participate in bidirectional cycles or invert the intended layering.

The schema-validation zone being completely decoupled from all production zones while the analysis pipeline writes output files is the highest-severity architectural gap in this batch — it means output correctness relies entirely on e2e tests rather than runtime enforcement.

The overall dependency flow (unit→analyzers, cli→analyzers, analyzers-2→analyzers, analyzers-3→analyzers) forms a correct hub-and-spoke pattern converging on the analysis-engine zone; the only violations of this pattern are the 7 edges flowing upward from analysis-engine into cli and unit zones.

The test-fixtures and schema-validation zones both show perfect cohesion (1.0) but for opposite architectural reasons: test-fixtures is self-contained by design, while schema-validation achieves perfect cohesion only because its sole production file is never imported into the pipeline, making the score misleading.

Across all five zones in this batch, no true circular dependencies exist between production modules — the bidirectional coupling metrics are inflated by test file co-location rather than runtime circular imports, which is an acceptable and common Louvain artifact.

The cleanest zone pair interface is file-analyzer-implementations → analysis-engine: 15 directional imports, 2 back, no shared mutable state suggested, clear consumer/provider split. This pair is a reference model for how the other tightly coupled analyzer zones should be refactored.

Three cross-cutting hub files (zones.ts, schema/index.ts, sort.ts) each imported by 4+ zones with no dedicated owner zone — a missing 'foundation' or 'core' zone is the root cause of their wide reach; creating one would reduce the number of zones affected by any single API change.

Triangular circular dependency spanning analysis-engine, analyze-pipeline-cli, and branch-workspace-reporting — all three zones are mutually reachable through import chains, constituting a system-level cycle that must be broken before zone boundary refactoring can proceed safely in any of the three zones.

The three analyzer overflow zones (analysis-engine, analyzers-2, analyzers-3) collectively exchange 30 cross-zone imports among themselves in addition to receiving imports from all other zones — treating them as a single logical domain with explicit sub-domain naming (e.g. enrich-pipeline, zone-detector, file-classifier) would convert implicit coupling into declared architecture.

Schema validation is architecturally isolated from the write path — the pipeline can produce and persist output files without ever calling validate.ts. Adding a single validate() call in the pipeline finalization step would promote validation from a test-time check to a runtime invariant.

All five zones in this batch have directional (not circular) import edges; the bidirectional coupling warnings in zone metrics are caused by test files co-located with production code inflating the Louvain community coupling score, not by runtime circular dependencies.

Test-tier import hierarchy is inverted: unit→e2e (2 edges) means unit-level test infrastructure depends on e2e-tier files — a shared test-support zone containing common fixtures would eliminate this inversion and give both tiers a stable import target.

The zone-detection-engine zone (low cohesion 0.27, high coupling 0.73) and the schema-validation zone (zero coupling, only e2e-exercised) represent the two opposite failure modes of zone design: one is too entangled with its neighbors, the other is too isolated from the runtime path it is meant to guard.

No zone in the codebase defines an explicit contract boundary (interface file, abstract class, or dedicated types-only module) for cross-zone communication — all 77 tracked cross-zone import edges connect concrete implementation files to concrete implementation files. This means the entire cross-zone API surface is implicit and refactor-unsafe.

The duplication between analysis-engine/completion-reader.ts and analyze-pipeline-cli/branch-work-collector.ts (acknowledged inline in the source) is the only documented case of cross-zone logic forking — it indicates a systemic pressure to copy rather than import when taking a full zone dependency feels too costly, which will worsen as coupling grows.

analyzers-2 ↔ analyzers-3 bidirectional exchange (2 imports) means the two overflow partitions of the analysis engine domain are not independent — resolving the triangular cycle (analysis-engine ↔ cli ↔ unit) will not fully decompose the engine cluster without also addressing the inter-overflow coupling.

The cross-zone import count 'cli → unit: 13 imports' reveals that the CLI layer has a hard structural dependency on the reporting/unit zone rather than consuming it through a stable interface — in a clean layered architecture the CLI should only depend on the analysis-engine zone and treat output formatting as a plugin or strategy, not a direct dependency.

No dedicated 'foundation' or 'core' zone exists to own the three hub files (zones.ts, schema/index.ts, sort.ts) that are each imported by 4+ zones — the absence of this zone is the root cause of the wide blast radius on any API change to these files; creating a foundation zone would make the dependency surface explicit and auditable.

The Louvain algorithm producing three overflow analyzer zones (analysis-engine, analyzers-2, analyzers-3) while the source files all live in src/analyzers/ is a strong signal that the source directory structure does not encode the intended architectural decomposition — the codebase needs subdirectory structure (e.g. src/analyzers/enrich/, src/analyzers/zones/, src/analyzers/output/) to give the community detection algorithm meaningful graph boundaries to cluster around.

The overall dependency graph has a correct hub-and-spoke shape (all zones import from the analysis-engine hub) but the 7 upward edges from analysis-engine into cli and unit zones create a cycle at the top of the hub — these 7 edges are the minimum cut needed to convert the current cyclic graph into a strict DAG, making them the highest-leverage refactoring targets in the entire codebase.

Output generation (llms.txt and CONTEXT.md writing) has no dedicated zone — based on the memory file this logic lives in analyze.ts within the analysis-engine zone, conflating pipeline orchestration with file I/O. Extracting output generation into its own zone would clarify the pipeline's stages and make the output format independently testable without running the full analysis pipeline.

All 77 cross-zone import edges connect concrete implementation files with no interface layer between them — the entire cross-zone API is implicit; no zone boundary can be safely refactored in isolation without auditing all inbound concrete imports first.

analyzers-2 imports from analyzers-3 (2 edges) — the two overflow partitions of the analysis engine are mutually coupled, making it impossible to extract either overflow zone independently without also resolving the other's dependencies.

Three hub utility files (src/analyzers/zones.ts, src/schema/index.ts, src/util/sort.ts) are each imported by 4+ zones but have no owner zone — they are orphaned cross-cutting concerns. Any breaking change to their APIs propagates to all dependents simultaneously with no single zone responsible for their stability. A dedicated 'foundation' zone owning these three files would make the dependency surface explicit and give them a clear maintainer.

The pipeline's output generation step (writing llms.txt and CONTEXT.md) is conflated with pipeline orchestration inside the analysis-engine zone. Output generation is a distinct concern from analysis computation — separating it into its own module or zone would allow output formats to evolve independently and enable integration tests to validate output shape without re-running the full analysis pipeline.

Cross-zone import 'cli → unit: 13 imports' shows the CLI has a structural hard dependency on the branch-workspace-reporting zone's internals rather than consuming a stable interface. The CLI should depend only on the analysis-engine public API and treat output formatting as a consumer-side concern, not a direct import dependency.

Zone naming conventions are inconsistent across the codebase: some zones use action-noun format ('analyze-pipeline-cli'), others use noun-noun ('analysis-engine', 'branch-workspace-reporting') — the absence of a naming standard means zone roles are not deducible from names alone and architectural discussions use inconsistent terminology.

Auto-generated zone names 'analyzers-2' and 'analyzers-3' are Louvain overflow artifacts encoding no semantic meaning — they cannot be referenced meaningfully in documentation, ADRs, or code comments; manually assigning names reflecting domain ownership (e.g. 'file-classifier-engine', 'zone-graph-detector') would give these zones architectural identity.

The gateway pattern (one gateway file per upstream package, re-exports only, enforced by domain-isolation.test.js) is documented and enforced for cross-package imports but has no analogous mechanism for cross-zone imports within a package — the 77 tracked intra-package cross-zone edges all connect concrete implementation files directly, meaning zone encapsulation is a social convention with no structural enforcement.

No zone in the codebase has a designated owner file (e.g., a zone-public.ts that explicitly declares the zone's cross-zone API surface) — the implicit convention is that every file in a zone is equally importable by other zones, making it impossible to programmatically enforce 'consumers must import only through zone interface' rules.

The [config] archetype (enrich-config.ts in analysis-engine) and [entrypoint] archetype (public.ts in branch-workspace-reporting) appear inside zones whose roles contradict those archetypes — archetypes that typically mark zone boundaries are embedded in non-boundary zones, indicating the archetype taxonomy is not being used as a zone membership signal.

There is no project-wide policy for whether test files co-locate with their production zone or reside in dedicated test zones — the e2e and fixtures zones demonstrate clean test isolation while branch-workspace-reporting hosts mixed production and test content; the inconsistency makes zone cohesion scores unreliable as indicators of production code structure.

Zone naming conventions are inconsistent across the codebase: zone-detection-engine uses a compound-noun-plus-role pattern, file-analyzer-implementations uses a domain-plus-type pattern, schema-validation uses a domain-plus-operation pattern, and test-fixtures uses a plain-noun pattern — no single convention exists. Inconsistent zone naming makes architectural documentation ambiguous and prevents tooling from inferring zone roles from names alone.

The two zones with perfect cohesion (schema-validation and test-fixtures) achieve that score for structurally opposite reasons: test-fixtures is self-contained by design, while schema-validation scores 1.0 only because its single production file is never imported outside the zone. Presenting both with the same cohesion score obscures a critical difference — one zone is healthy, the other is isolated from the runtime path it is meant to guard.

The most algorithmically complex zone (zone-detection-engine, containing the Louvain community detection implementation) is also the most fragile zone by combined cohesion+coupling metrics. Complexity and fragility co-locating in the same zone amplifies refactoring risk: changes needed to improve zone quality are hardest to make safely in the zone that most needs them.

No zone in the codebase enforces its public API through an index or barrel file that explicitly declares what is exported vs. what is internal — all 77 cross-zone import edges reach into concrete implementation files. Without explicit public surfaces, every file in every zone is implicitly public, making safe zone boundary refactoring impossible without auditing all consumers.

The four zones analyzed in this batch represent four distinct failure modes of zone design: file-analyzer-implementations (correct grouping but wrong boundary for the entrypoint), zone-detection-engine (fragile: low cohesion + high coupling), schema-validation (isolated: correct internally but disconnected from runtime path), test-fixtures (healthy: self-contained by design). Documenting these archetypes would give developers a reference vocabulary for diagnosing zone health issues in future analysis passes.

Rename auto-generated zones 'analyzers-2' and 'analyzers-3' to semantically meaningful names reflecting their domain (e.g. 'file-classifier-engine', 'zone-graph-detector') — numeric-suffix names prevent zone-based architectural communication and make it impossible to reference these zones in documentation or discussions without ambiguity.

Define a project-wide policy for test file zone membership: either tests always co-locate with their production zone or tests always live in dedicated test zones — the current mixed state (e2e/fixtures zones isolated, branch-workspace-reporting mixed) makes cohesion and coupling scores incomparable across zones and masks production-only coupling.

Introduce an intra-package zone interface convention analogous to the cross-package gateway pattern: each zone exposes a zone-public.ts that re-exports only its intended cross-zone API, with a lint rule or test enforcing that external zones import only from zone-public.ts rather than from internal implementation files.

Adopt a consistent zone naming convention — current names mix action-noun ('analyze-pipeline-cli'), adjective-compound-noun ('branch-workspace-reporting'), and noun-noun ('analysis-engine') patterns; standardizing on a single schema (e.g. '<domain>-<architectural-role>') would make zone roles immediately readable from name alone.

Establish and document a zone naming convention before the next analysis pass — proposed convention: '{domain}-{role}' where role is one of: engine (autonomous, low coupling), service (concrete impl), pipeline (orchestration), foundation (shared utilities), fixtures (test data). Applying this convention retroactively would make zone health metrics (especially coupling scores) interpretable without reading zone file contents.

The codebase has no zone with coupling < 0.3 and cohesion > 0.7 among production zones (only test zones achieve clean scores) — this indicates that no production zone has yet been refactored to a stable, well-bounded state. The target architectural state should include at least one reference zone with clean metrics that other zones can emulate.

All 77 cross-zone import edges connect concrete files with no interface layer — introduce at least one explicit contract boundary (a types-only or interface-only module) at the analysis-engine zone boundary to validate that the pattern is feasible before attempting to apply it to all zones. This single change would demonstrate the pattern and immediately reduce the blast radius of any analysis-engine API change.

Reverse imports from analyzers into the cli and unit zones (7 + 5 edges) suggest mild layering inversions; auditing these imports and pushing shared types down to a foundation module would improve the dependency graph.
High coupling (0.6) — 29 imports target "analysis-engine"

Branch-work utilities (classifier, filter, store) and risk-scoring form a coherent sub-cluster within this zone; they could be extracted into a dedicated 'branch-risk-analysis' zone if the codebase grows.

Coupling score of 0.6 meets the warning threshold; the zone imports from analyzers (29), cli (3), analyzers-2 (2), analyzers-3 (3), and e2e (2), indicating it acts as a cross-cutting hub rather than a focused module.

Mixing src/public.ts (the package's public API entrypoint), schema type files, and unit test helpers in one community suggests the Louvain graph does not yet have enough edge weight to separate these concerns; manually anchoring schema and public API into a dedicated zone would help.

Zero coupling to other zones is architecturally clean for a validation module; this makes schema validation portable and easy to test in isolation.

Cohesion of 0.27 is below the 0.4 threshold — the three production files (louvain.ts, zone-hash.ts, zones.ts) may not be tightly enough related to warrant their own zone; merging with the 'Analysis Engine' zone should be evaluated.

Coupling of 0.73 exceeds the 0.6 warning threshold, indicating heavy dependency on other zones; the zone-detection logic is not self-contained and relies significantly on the broader analysis infrastructure.
Zone "analysis-engine" has files across 9 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Zone name 'analysis-engine' does not match its source directory 'src/analyzers/' — align them by either renaming the zone to 'analyzers-core' or restructuring source files under 'src/analysis-engine/' so IDE navigation, zone references in documentation, and Louvain clustering inputs are all consistent.

claude-client.ts provides direct LLM API access from within the core computation zone with no interface abstraction — extract an ILLMClient interface and inject it as a dependency so unit tests can substitute a stub and the analysis engine zone no longer carries an implicit external network dependency.

convergence.ts is a stateless pure-function algorithm placed inside the CLI orchestration zone — move it to the analysis-engine zone or a shared foundation module so its logic is reachable without a CLI layer dependency, and the CLI zone's semantic boundary remains limited to command orchestration.

export-pdf.ts and git-credential-helper.ts have no relationship to the analyze pipeline lifecycle — their presence violates the zone's stated semantic scope; move them to an 'ancillary-cli-commands' zone or equivalent to preserve the zone's architectural focus and prevent the 'analyze-pipeline' name from becoming misleading.
Zone "branch-workspace-reporting" has files across 10 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

This zone has BOTH high coupling (0.6) AND low cohesion (0.4) — the only zone in this batch to exhibit both simultaneously. Prioritize splitting: move src/public.ts and schema types to a dedicated zone, and move branch-work utilities to a 'branch-risk-analysis' zone, to break the fragile catch-all structure before further features are added.

src/public.ts co-located with branch-workspace internals forces every package consumer to transitively couple to this high-coupling zone — extract public.ts to its own dedicated zone (e.g. 'package-public-api') so the public API surface is independent of the branch reporting internals and can be changed without affecting branch-workspace coupling metrics.

src/analyzers/ contains files from 5 zones (analysis-engine, analyze-pipeline-cli, branch-workspace-reporting, file-analyzer-implementations, zone-detection-engine) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries

This single change would demonstrate the pattern and immediately reduce the blast radius of any analysis-engine API change.

Applying this convention retroactively would make zone health metrics (especially coupling scores) interpretable without reading zone file contents.

The target architectural state should include at least one reference zone with clean metrics that other zones can emulate.

Zone "test-fixtures" has files across 5 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Add a fixture-to-analyzer coverage matrix (even as a comment in tests/fixtures/index.ts) mapping each fixture to the analyzers it exercises — currently there is no documented link between fixture files and the analyzer features they cover, making it unclear when adding a new analyzer feature whether a new fixture is needed.

Establish a naming convention that encodes fixture purpose in the directory name (e.g. tests/fixtures/route-detection-remix/, tests/fixtures/import-graph-ts/) rather than relying on implicit knowledge — the current names 'remix-app' and 'small-ts-project' describe technology stack, not the analyzer scenario being exercised.

Cohesion of 0.65 is the strongest among the non-trivial zones, confirming that the CLI orchestration layer is well-organized around the analyze pipeline lifecycle.

Three reverse imports (unit → cli) create a minor cycle between the CLI and branch-workspace-reporting zones; identifying which unit-zone files import from CLI and moving any shared symbols to a lower layer would eliminate the cycle.
git-credential-helper.ts and export-pdf.ts are ancillary features within the CLI zone; if they grow in complexity they are good candidates for extraction into dedicated feature zones.

Production utilities (branch-work-*.ts, risk-scoring.ts) cohabiting with test helpers in the same zone causes the analyze-pipeline-cli zone to transitively depend on test infrastructure through its 13 inbound imports — separating production utilities from test helpers would break this unwanted coupling.
The 15 outbound imports to analysis-engine and 2 inbound form a clean one-directional consumer relationship — no circular dependency exists between these two zones.

validate.ts has zero import edges to or from any production zone; it is only exercised through e2e and integration tests. If the analysis pipeline does not call validate() at the end of each run, malformed output files can reach consumers silently.

The two fixture sub-projects (remix-app, small-ts-project) are unrelated datasets with no shared imports; grouping them in one zone is a filesystem artifact. If a third fixture is added, consider a fixtures/index.ts manifest to keep fixture discovery centralized.

Three separate unit test files (zone-detection, zone-size-policy, zone-subdivision) map to distinct sub-domains within two production files — the test surface reveals a hidden split that should be made explicit in source organization before these concerns grow further.

analysis-engine, analyze-pipeline-cli, and branch-workspace-reporting form a triangular circular dependency: analyzers→cli (7), cli→unit (13), unit→analyzers (29), analyzers→unit (5), unit→cli (3), cli→analyzers (20). All three zones are mutually reachable — this is a system-level cycle, not just three isolated pairwise inversions, and cannot be resolved by refactoring any single zone alone.

High internal cohesion (0.65) coexists with active participation in circular dependencies — cohesion and external coupling are orthogonal properties; the CLI zone's boundary health requires coupling analysis, not cohesion metrics, to diagnose.

route-detection.ts has no import edges to sibling analyzer files in this zone; it is grouped by filesystem proximity only and should be extracted to a dedicated route-analysis zone once zone boundaries are tightened.

The schema-validation zone is correctly isolated from analyzers and cli zones for portability, but the absence of any inbound import from the pipeline entrypoint means validation is opt-in rather than enforced — a single import from the pipeline finalization step would close this gap.

Zero inbound edges from any production zone confirms correct isolation; verify that fixture paths are referenced via a shared constant in test helpers rather than duplicated path strings to reduce coupling to the filesystem layout.

zone-hash.ts has no outbound edges to zones outside zone-detection-engine; if it is only consumed within this zone it is a private module incorrectly treated as a zone boundary and should be marked internal.

Zone exposes 19 concrete implementation files as its API surface with no facade or interface module — consumer zones are forced to import internal files directly, creating maximum blast radius for any internal restructuring.

completion-reader.ts explicitly documents itself as a local copy of logic from branch-work-collector.ts — duplicated cross-zone logic with no enforcement mechanism to keep the two in sync; any fix to the original will silently miss the copy.

branch-work-collector.ts and convergence.ts are domain services (not CLI commands) placed in the CLI zone — non-CLI consumers cannot reach these services without taking a transitive dependency on the CLI orchestration layer.

pr-markdown.ts (CLI command) directly imports branch-work-collector.ts from the analyzers path while branch-work-collector lives in the analyze-pipeline-cli zone — the import path and the zone boundary are misaligned, hiding that this is a CLI-to-CLI intra-zone call rather than a CLI-to-domain cross-zone call.

branch-work-collector.ts is the canonical owner of parsing logic that has already been duplicated into analysis-engine/completion-reader.ts — hosting authoritative domain logic in a high-coupling catch-all zone creates pressure to fork rather than import, accelerating divergence.

src/util/paths.ts is a utility misclassified into the analyzer-implementations zone by import affinity. As a cross-cutting utility imported by multiple zones, it belongs in a dedicated foundation or utility zone alongside sort.ts and schema/index.ts — the three hub utilities should share an owner zone.

E2e and integration test files are co-located in the same Louvain community because both import validate.ts — CI parallelism and test reporting depend on these tiers being separately categorized. Mixing them in one zone obscures which test tier caught a regression.

Zone contains 1 production file and 4 test files (80% test) — the zone boundary is a test-grouping artifact, not a domain boundary. The single production file (validate.ts) has no identity as a 'zone' and should be absorbed into a shared schema or foundation zone where it can be imported by the pipeline.

Fixture paths are consumed as string literals scattered across test files in multiple zones rather than through a centralized fixture registry or path-constants module. A single tests/fixtures/index.ts exporting named fixture paths would make relocation atomic and prevent path drift.

zones.ts is exercised by three separate unit test files each covering a distinct algorithmic concern (detection, size policy, subdivision) — this indicates zones.ts has at least three separate responsibilities. Splitting it into zones-detect.ts, zones-size-policy.ts, and zones-subdivide.ts would align source organization with the already-expressed test structure and improve zone cohesion from 0.27.

Rename the zone from 'file-analyzer-implementations' to 'file-analyzers' or 'analyzer-services' — the 'implementations' suffix implies a corresponding interface layer that does not exist, creating a misleading architectural signal for anyone onboarding to the codebase.

src/analyzers/index.ts (entrypoint archetype) belongs in the analysis-engine zone that owns the public API, not the implementations zone — relocating it would give the public API surface a clear owner and prevent the entrypoint from being refactored as if it were a private implementation detail.

Absorb validate.ts into a shared 'schema' or 'foundation' zone alongside schema/index.ts so the production file gains a meaningful owner zone, and move the e2e/integration tests back to their respective test-tier zones — this eliminates the misleading zone name and fixes the mixed-tier test co-location in one step.

Cohesion=1.0 for a single-file production zone is a degenerate metric, not an architectural achievement — sourcevision should suppress or annotate cohesion scores for zones with fewer than 3 production files to prevent false confidence in zone health.

Rename the zone to 'zone-detection' or 'louvain-clustering' to accurately reflect its dependent sub-module role — 'engine' implies autonomy that the coupling metrics explicitly contradict, and the misleading name may cause developers to treat it as a stable boundary when it is not.

zone-detection-engine has both low cohesion (0.27) and high coupling (0.73) — this is the highest-fragility zone in the codebase. Any refactor of the analysis-engine zone will cascade here. Prioritize merging louvain.ts and zone-hash.ts into the analysis-engine zone or extracting them into a named sub-package before any broader architectural work begins.
